summary: I was born in Hiroshima Prefecture as the first son of a poor farmer. Life there was hard, especially during the Sino-Japanese War when taxes increased and common people struggled. I remember walking to Hiroshima city when I was about 7 or 8 years old to visit a neighbor wounded in the Russo-Japanese War. The countryside was hilly, and we mostly grew rice and wheat in the winter. My grandmother raised me there until I came to America at age 13.

My father went to Hawaii before I was born, around 1895 or earlier, as an immigrant to grow sugar beets under a three-year contract. After his contract ended, he returned to Japan and married my mother. Later, my uncle also went to Hawaii as a contract immigrant before coming to America. Hawaii was a common first destination for Japanese immigrants before moving on to the mainland United States.

After my father left Japan again, he went to Canada before coming to America. He left my mother and me behind in Japan when he went to Canada at age 42. Life was difficult in Japan during the Sino-Japanese War, which likely influenced his decision to leave. From Canada, he eventually moved to the United States. This journey was part of the immigrant experience for many Japanese seeking better opportunities.
cues: father went to Canada after Japan

summary: The Sino-Japanese War from 1894 to 1895 was a difficult time for farmers like my family in Japan. The government raised taxes to pay for armaments, which made life very hard for common people, though the rich landowners did well. This economic strain was one reason my father left Japan to seek work abroad. I remember hearing about the war's impact on our village and how it shaped our family's struggles.

When I was about 7 or 8 years old, I visited a neighbor who was wounded and staying in an Army hospital in Hiroshima due to the Russo-Japanese War. That memory stands out because I got separated from my grandmother on the way home and cried. The war was a significant event in our community, and many men went off to fight. It was a time when discipline was strict even in schools, preparing boys to become soldiers.

Hiroshima city was about five miles from my village, and I remember walking there as a child to visit a wounded neighbor during the Russo-Japanese War. It was a place where I went for medical treatment before coming to America. Only a few houses on the main street in Hiroshima had electricity when I was young. The city was a central point in my early life and memories.

When I left Japan to come to America in December 1910, there was no pier in Kobe, so we had to take a rowboat to board the ship. The ship was small, about 6,000 tons, and the two-week voyage was difficult for me because I was used to fresh air and fresh food. Kobe was the port from which I departed on my journey to the United States.

The transcript does not provide specific details about Yokkaichi in my story. I do not recall mentioning Yokkaichi or having direct experiences related to it during my childhood or immigration journey.

I did not mention Yokohama specifically in my story. My departure was from Kobe, and my journey took me to Seattle and San Francisco in America, but Yokohama was not part of my personal experience as I recall it.

After arriving in America, I was told to go to Seattle because the physical examination at the Immigration office in San Francisco was stricter. It took me about two weeks to get there by ship. Seattle was my first destination in the United States after leaving Japan, marking the beginning of my new life here.

San Francisco was the place where the immigration physical examination was stricter, so I was advised to go to Seattle instead. Although I did not stay long in San Francisco, it was part of my immigration process when I first arrived in America in 1910. The experience highlighted the challenges Asian immigrants faced entering the United States.
